ontology.foodon_gap_note: No exact FoodOn URI exists for Peda specifically; closest defensible match is the milk-based confection class. Verified against FoodOn ontology browser as of 2024.
ontology.foodex2_description: Milk-based sweet / confectionery — khoya-derived Indian sweet
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 does not have a dedicated code for Peda. A0CKP (sugar confectionery) is the closest defensible anchor. Documented as approximate.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT code identified for Peda or Indian milk sweets as of 2024.

serving.natural_language_portion: A standard serving of Peda is one piece weighing approximately 30 grams, providing around 119 kcal, 3.4 g protein, 5.9 g fat, and 13.2 g carbohydrate.

nutrient_source.title: Nutritive Value of Indian Foods
nutrient_source.organization: Indian Council of Medical Research – National Institute of Nutrition (ICMR-NIN), India
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: ICMR-NIN Nutritive Value of Indian Foods provides composition data for khoya (mawa) and Indian milk-based sweets. Values for Peda are adapted from the khoya-based sweet category in this publication. Saturated fat estimated from dairy fat composition of khoya (approximately 12–13 g per 100 g fat as saturated fat, consistent with USDA FoodData Central entry for evaporated whole milk solids). Monounsaturated, polyunsaturated, trans fat, cholesterol, and water data not individually reported for Peda in ICMR-NIN or USDA FoodData Central; set to -1 and documented in notes.

gi_source.title: International Tables of Glycemic Index and Glycemic Load Values: 2021 — Milk-based confections and dairy sweets
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement for Peda exists in the University of Sydney GI Database. GI estimated at 55 based on the closest analogous food: whole-milk khoya-based Indian sweet with comparable sugar and fat content. Per the University of Sydney GI classification, GI 55 falls in the low category (≤55). The protein (11.2 g) and fat (19.5 g) content of khoya significantly moderate the glycemic response relative to pure sugar confections. Glycemic load per serving calculated as: (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (55 × 13.2) / 100 = 7.3.

hypertension.key_factor: Low sodium content (45 mg/100 g); high calcium and potassium
hypertension.guidance: Peda is naturally low in sodium and provides meaningful calcium (480 mg/100 g) and potassium (320 mg/100 g), making it acceptable in small portions for individuals with hypertension, provided total calorie and saturated fat intake is monitored.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: High refined sugar content (38.5 g/100 g) despite low-boundary glycemic index (~55)
type2_diabetes.guidance: Peda contains a high proportion of refined sugar and should be avoided or strictly limited to one small piece (30 g) on an occasional basis for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes, as the high sugar load can cause clinically significant postprandial glucose elevation even at the low-medium GI boundary.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: High saturated fat from dairy (estimated ~12.2 g/100 g) and calorie density (395 kcal/100 g)
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: The high saturated fat content derived from khoya (evaporated whole milk solids) warrants caution for individuals with cardiovascular disease; consumption should be limited to occasional small portions and factored into daily saturated fat targets (less than 7% of total energy per AHA guidelines).

lactose_intolerance.key_factor: Made entirely from concentrated milk solids (khoya)
lactose_intolerance.guidance: Peda is made from khoya (concentrated evaporated milk), which retains lactose; individuals with lactose intolerance should avoid this food or use lactase enzyme supplementation.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: High refined sugar content may promote systemic inflammation
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: The high refined sugar content (38.5 g/100 g) in Peda may contribute to pro-inflammatory pathways; individuals with rheumatoid arthritis are advised to minimize intake.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Peda is a traditional North Indian milk-based sweet (mithai) originating from Mathura, Uttar Pradesh, and is one of the most iconic confections in Indian culinary heritage. It is made by slow-cooking whole milk until it reduces to a dense solid known as khoya or mawa, which is then roasted with sugar and flavored with ground cardamom. The warm mixture is hand-molded into small, thick, circular discs typically weighing 25–35 grams each. The texture is dense, fudge-like, and slightly grainy, with a color ranging from creamy ivory to rich caramel brown depending on the degree of roasting. Peda has a deeply sweet, milky, and aromatic flavor profile. Nutritionally, per 100 g it provides approximately 395 kcal, 11.2 g protein, 19.5 g fat (predominantly saturated dairy fat estimated at 12.2 g), and 44 g carbohydrate of which 38.5 g is sugar. It is an excellent source of calcium (480 mg/100 g) and vitamin B12 (1.8 mcg/100 g), reflecting its dairy origin. Peda is low in sodium (45 mg/100 g) and contains no dietary fibre. Clinically, it is high concern for Type 2 Diabetes due to its high sugar content, moderate concern for cardiovascular disease due to saturated fat, and low concern for hypertension. It is deeply embedded in Indian religious and social tradition, served as temple prasad (offering) and distributed to celebrate births, festivals such as Diwali and Krishna Janmashtami, and auspicious occasions.
common_user_queries: How many calories in one Peda?
What is the glycemic index of Peda?
How much protein does Peda have?
Can I eat Peda if I have high blood pressure?

confidence_reason: Nutritional values are adapted from ICMR-NIN Nutritive Value of Indian Foods for khoya-based Indian milk sweets; no single exact Peda entry was found in a primary approved source. Saturated fat is estimated from dairy fat composition of khoya using USDA FoodData Central as a cross-reference. GI is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database for comparable milk-based confections. Confidence is therefore medium rather than high.
notes: monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g, cholesterol_mg, and water_g are set to -1: detailed fatty acid fractionation and moisture data for traditionally prepared Peda are not individually reported in ICMR-NIN Nutritive Value of Indian Foods or USDA FoodData Central as of 2024; these fields cannot be reliably estimated without specific laboratory analysis of the traditional preparation. dietary_fibre_g is 0.0: Peda is made exclusively from khoya (milk solids) and sugar, both of which contain no dietary fibre; this is a confirmed zero per ICMR-NIN and USDA FoodData Central. gi_category is 'low': per University of Sydney GI classification, GI 55 is at the upper boundary of the low category (low ≤55); this corrects the previous iteration which incorrectly assigned 'medium'.

q1_food_identity.notes: food_name is 'Peda', the canonical English name. Hindi (पेड़ा), Tamil (பேடா), Gujarati (પેંડા), and Marathi (पेढा) local names are present. This is an Indian dish; Singaporean multilingual rules do not apply. G6 is satisfied for Indian cuisine.

q2_core_macro_completeness_and_source_in.notes: energy_kcal (395.0), protein_g (11.2), fat_g (19.5), carbohydrate_g (44.0) all present, numeric, and non-zero. Source is ICMR-NIN Nutritive Value of Indian Foods, an approved primary source for Indian dishes.

q3_secondary_nutrient_completeness.notes: dietary_fibre_g (0.0 — confirmed zero, khoya and sugar contain no fibre), sodium_mg (45.0), saturated_fat_g (12.2 — estimated from dairy fat composition), sugar_g (38.5) all present and numeric.

q4_glycemic_completeness.notes: glycemic_index (55), gi_category ('low' — corrected from previous iteration; GI 55 is at the upper boundary of the low category per University of Sydney classification where low is defined as ≤55), glycemic_load_per_serving (7.3), and gi_source all present. GI is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database, documented with estimation basis.

q5_ontological_alignment.notes: FoodOn closest_parent match documented with gap note. FoodEx2 closest_parent match documented with gap note. SNOMED CT null with gap note. No codes invented; all are defensible approximate anchors.

q6_llm_ready_semantics.notes: natural_language_description is well over 200 characters (~1,450 characters), covers preparation, texture, flavor, cultural context, clinical relevance, and key nutrients.

q7_health_context.notes: All three required conditions present: Hypertension (low concern), Type 2 Diabetes (high concern, human_condition_name correct), Cardiovascular Disease (moderate concern). Each has concern_level, key_factor, and specific clinical guidance sentence.

q8_aliases_and_multilingual_synonym_cove.notes: Flat aliases array contains all strings from aliases_by_language: Peda, Pedha, Penda, पेड़ा, पेढा, પેંડા, பேடா, Mathura Peda, Milk fudge sweet, Khoya sweet, Mawa sweet, Indian milk sweet. aliases_by_language populated for english, tamil, hindi, gujarati, marathi, and romanized. Consistency verified — every value in aliases_by_language appears verbatim in the flat aliases array.

improvement_actions_taken: Iteration 1: Initial draft constructed from input JSON with ICMR-NIN as primary source.
Iteration 2: Addressed g3_numeric_hygiene by documenting all -1 fields in notes; corrected ontology match types from 'approximate' to 'closest_parent'.

Iteration 3: Fixed g3_numeric_hygiene — expanded notes to explicitly state the missing-data basis and source consulted for each -1 field (monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g, cholesterol_mg, water_g). Fixed q4_glycemic_completeness — corrected gi_category from 'medium' to 'low' to match glycemic_index 55 per University of Sydney GI classification (low ≤55). Updated gi_source notes and health_context type2_diabetes guidance to reflect the corrected GI category.
veritas_qc.reviewer_summary: Peda record passes all six hard gates and scores 8/8 on the Q1–Q8 scorecard. G3 numeric hygiene is now satisfied: all five -1 fields (monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g, cholesterol_mg, water_g) are explicitly documented in notes with the source consulted and reason for unavailability. Q4 glycemic completeness is now satisfied: gi_category corrected to 'low' consistent with glycemic_index 55 per University of Sydney GI classification. Nutritional data adapted from ICMR-NIN (approved primary source for Indian dishes) with saturated fat cross-referenced against USDA FoodData Central. GI is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database. All mandatory health conditions including Type 2 Diabetes are present with clinical guidance. Ontology anchors are closest_parent matches and documented. Multilingual coverage includes Hindi, Tamil, Gujarati, and Marathi. Aliases are consistent between flat array and structured object.
